prompt: If photosynthesis had evolved to release chlorine gas instead of oxygen, how would Earth's biosphere plausibly differ today? Think it through; don't just list bullet points.
response:
Imagine the first faint whiff of chlorine drifting out of a bacterial mat 3 billion years ago.  
The air is still mostly nitrogen and CO₂, but now a pale greenish thread curls above the water, acrid even at a few parts per million. Nothing has yet evolved a nose to complain, yet everything is already being rewritten.

1.  The immediate chemical revolution  
    Chlorine is a powerful oxidant, but it does not stay naked for long. It attacks the nearest reductant—dissolved ferrous iron, sulfide, ammonium, organic grime—producing chloride and oxidised side-products. In seawater the first result is a thin sun-lit “redox shelf”: above it chlorine, below it chloride. The ocean’s total inventory of oxidising power rises until every dissolved Fe²⁺ has become Fe³⁺ and precipitated as banded iron formations. Once that buffer is gone, the gas begins to accumulate in earnest. Because the photolysis of chlorine in the stratosphere makes Cl· radicals that destroy the very H₂O molecules that could have shielded them, the upper atmosphere dries out. Earth keeps its nitrogen blanket, but water vapour is capped at a lower mixing ratio; the planet’s infrared thermostat shifts downward a few degrees. A faint chlorine-green aurora replaces the rosy glow of oxygen.

2.  The first biological casualties and survivors  
    Any microbe whose external chemistry is even mildly reducing—iron-breathers, sulfate reducers, methanogens—finds its cell surface literally burned. Only three refuges remain:  
    – Buried sediments where pore-water chloride is the only halogen and chlorine never penetrates.  
    – Acidic seeps where dissolved chloride is so high that Cl₂ solubility collapses (the way CO₂ fizzes out of champagne).  
    – Inside another cell. Endosymbiosis is no longer a rare adventure; it is the only way to keep your DNA away from the green death.  
    The winners are the clade we might call “perchlorophores.” Their outer membrane is sheathed in an aromatised carbon polymer resembling halogenated humus—chlorine’s own Teflon. Instead of guarding against oxidation they harness it: they let chlorine diffuse in through controlled porins, immediately reduce it to Cl⁻ with an organic donor, and use the liberated electrons to drive CO₂ fixation via a reversed chloride cycle. ATP is made by a chemiosmotic Cl⁻/Cl₂ couple rather than H⁺/O₂. The waste product, happily, is more chloride—harmless, soluble, already the ocean’s dominant anion.

3.  Multicellularity on a toxic planet  
    Macroscopic life has to solve two problems at once: (i) keep chlorine out of the wet interior and (ii) deliver CO₂ to the chlorophyll that needs
